policy_statement: Build a 132 kV 'Elba–Continente' power line and a 150 kV interconnection for the Campania islands to ensure adequate safety, continuity and efficiency of local service.
mechanism_description: Builds new high-voltage power line and interconnection infrastructure to improve the safety, continuity, and efficiency of local electricity service.
primary_causal_pathway: Same electricity service delivered → ↓ grid losses/curtailment & improved dispatch → ↓ generation set per kWh delivered
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Invest in/construct higher-capacity or higher-voltage transmission lines and interconnectors (and associated substations/control equipment) to strengthen grid connections between areas. (2) Mediating process: Higher-voltage/less-constrained networks reduce resistive losses, relieve congestion, reduce inefficient dispatch and renewable curtailment, and improve power flow management. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Less total electricity generation is needed to deliver the same end-use electricity service; this lowers system energy waste and typically reduces GHG emissions intensity/quantity (depending on the marginal generation mix).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing electricity system losses/congestion so the same delivered electricity requires less generation (and thus potentially fewer associated emissions).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: transmission/interconnection upgrades → reduced losses/congestion and improved operational efficiency → less generation needed for the same delivered electricity.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Mitigation outcomes (e.g., enabling renewables) and adaptation outcomes (improved outage resilience) may occur, but they are not guaranteed for every interconnection upgrade; efficiency/loss reduction is the most universal, direct physical effect across instances.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific project is primarily justified/engineered for climate resilience (e.g., hardening against wildfire/flood) or explicitly to integrate large new renewable capacity, the dominant pathway could shift at the policy level—but that would require policy-specific evidence beyond this mechanism definition.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any grid interconnection upgrade inherently alters power flows and typically reduces losses or congestion relative to the counterfactual, making “same service with less system energy waste” the consistent primary pathway.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary classification (e.g., mitigation via renewable integration; adaptation via reliability under hazards) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some interconnections can increase total delivered electricity and emissions if they facilitate higher fossil generation exports/imports; others are primarily for resilience/reliability rather than efficiency. If the stated primary intent and design is renewable integration or hazard hardening, the dominant category could plausibly shift to Mitigation or Adaptation for that specific policy.
key_indicators: "Build a 132 kV 'Elba–Continente' power line"; "150 kV interconnection"; "ensure ... continuity and efficiency of the local service"
co_benefits: Improved grid reliability/resilience and safety of electricity supply for islands/remote areas; potential reduction in outage-related economic losses.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a grid planning/expansion mandate, but the text is explicitly about constructing named assets, so infrastructure investment is the best fit.
